skill.name: godot-gdscript-patterns
skill.description: Master Godot 4 GDScript patterns including signals, scenes, state machines, and optimization. Use when building Godot games, implementing game systems, or learning GDScript best practices.
skill.summary: Production-ready patterns for Godot 4 GDScript development
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 60 potential security issues, all confirmed as false positives after review. All 'external_commands' findings are markdown code fence delimiters in GDScript examples. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings refer to Godot 4's built-in encrypted file API (FileAccess.open_encrypted_with_pass) used legitimately in save system examples. 'Generic API keys' finding is a placeholder encryption key in example code (your_secret_key_here). 'Network' findings are documentation links to legitimate Godot resources. This skill contains only educational GDScript code examples and documentation with no executable content or security risks.

content.value_statement: Building Godot games without proper architecture leads to unmaintainable code and performance issues. This skill provides production-ready GDScript patterns including state machines, component systems, object pooling, and save systems to help you build clean, scalable games.
actual_capabilities: Generate state machine implementations for game entities
Create component-based architecture for game objects
Implement object pooling for performance optimization
Build autoload singletons for global game state
limitations: Only covers Godot 4.x (not compatible with Godot 3.x)
Patterns are focused on 2D games (though most apply to 3D)
Does not cover multiplayer networking patterns
Save system examples use placeholder encryption keys that need replacement

use_cases.title: Implement Player State Machine
use_cases.description: Create a state machine for player character with idle, move, attack, and jump states. Use this when building platformers or action games where player behavior changes based on game state.
use_cases.target_user: Indie game developer building a 2D platformer

use_cases.title: Object Pooling for Bullets
use_cases.description: Implement object pooling for frequently spawned objects like bullets or projectiles. Use this to reduce garbage collection hitches and improve performance in games with many spawned entities.
use_cases.target_user: Game developer optimizing performance for shoot-em-up games

use_cases.title: Component-Based Health System
use_cases.description: Create reusable health, hitbox, and hurtbox components that can be attached to any game entity. Use this when building games with many characters that need combat functionality.
use_cases.target_user: Game developer creating modular combat system for RPG

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic State Machine
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a state machine for a [ENTITY_TYPE] with states: [LIST_STATES]. Include the StateMachine class, State base class, and one concrete state implementation.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner implementing their first state machine pattern

prompt_templates.title: Implement Object Pool
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an object pool system for [OBJECT_TYPE] that pre-instantiates [NUMBER] objects and can grow dynamically. Include the pool script and a pooled object script with spawn/despawn callbacks.

prompt_templates.title: Build Component System
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a component system with [COMPONENT_1] and [COMPONENT_2] that interact via signals. Each component should be standalone and attachable to any node.

prompt_templates.title: Create Save System with Encryption
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a save system that saves [DATA_TYPES] to an encrypted file. Include the SaveManager autoload and a Saveable component for individual objects.
prompt_templates.scenario: Experienced developer building persistent game state

output_examples.input: Create a state machine for an enemy with Idle, Patrol, and Chase states
output_examples.output: Generates StateMachine class (extends Node), State base class, and three concrete state scripts: EnemyIdle, EnemyPatrol, and EnemyChase. Includes transition logic based on player distance detection and signal connections for state changes.

output_examples.input: Create an object pool for bullets with 20 initial instances
output_examples.output: Provides ObjectPool script with get_instance() and return methods, plus PooledBullet script with lifetime management, velocity-based movement, and returned_to_pool signal. Includes initialization code that pre-creates 20 disabled bullet instances.

best_practices: Cache node references with @onready instead of calling get_node() in process loops
Use signals for communication between nodes to avoid tight coupling
Separate data into Resources (extending Resource) and keep logic in Nodes
Use static typing on all variables and function parameters for better performance
Disable processing on nodes when not needed (set_process, set_physics_process)
anti_patterns: Do not call get_node() or $ notation in _process() or _physics_process() loops
Do not store direct node references across scenes - use signals or autoloads
Do not put game logic in Resource scripts - keep them as data containers
Do not create new objects in hot code paths - use object pooling instead
Do not use string names for signals when you can use Callable with type safety

faq.question: What Godot version does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill supports Godot 4.x only. GDScript syntax and APIs differ significantly from Godot 3.x, so patterns may not work in earlier versions.

faq.question: Can I use these patterns for 3D games?
faq.answer: Most patterns apply to both 2D and 3D. State machines, components, object pooling, and save systems work identically. Only specific node types (like Area2D) need to be changed to their 3D equivalents (Area3D).

faq.question: How do I replace the placeholder encryption key?
faq.answer: Generate a cryptographically secure random string and replace 'your_secret_key_here' in the ENCRYPTION_KEY constant. Use a key derivation function if you need to derive it from a user password. Never commit real keys to version control.

faq.question: Should I use autoloads for all my global data?
faq.answer: No, use autoloads sparingly. Only use them for truly global systems like game managers, event buses, or scene managers. For most data, prefer Resources or dependency injection through signals.

faq.question: Why use component-based architecture over inheritance?
faq.answer: Components allow you to mix and match functionality like health, movement, and combat across different characters without deep inheritance hierarchies. They make code more reusable and easier to maintain.

faq.question: When should I use object pooling?
faq.answer: Use object pooling for objects that spawn frequently and quickly (bullets, particles, enemies). Pooling reduces garbage collection spikes and improves performance. For objects that spawn rarely (like power-ups), pooling is usually unnecessary.
